annotations.label: Curvature (intuitive)

anchor.snippet: curvature is any of several strongly related concepts in geometry that intuitively measure the amount by which a curve deviates from being a straight line

annotations.note: No curvature concepts found in Mathlib4 (only an incidental comment mention in Mathlib/MeasureTheory/Measure/Doubling.lean).

annotations.label: Curvature of a curve as rate of change of tangent direction

anchor.snippet: Curvature measures the angular rate of change of the direction of the tangent line

annotations.note: Mathlib4 has no formalization of curve curvature via tangent angular rate.

annotations.label: Curvature at a point via osculating circle

anchor.snippet: The curvature at a point on a twice differentiable curve is the magnitude of its curvature vector at that point

annotations.note: Grep finds no osculating-circle or curvature-vector definitions in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Maximal, minimal, and mean curvature for surfaces

anchor.snippet: This leads to the concepts of maximal curvature , minimal curvature , and mean curvature

annotations.note: No principal/mean surface curvature concepts in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Cauchy: center of curvature as intersection of normals

anchor.snippet: Augustin-Louis Cauchy showed that the center of curvature is the intersection point of two infinitely close normal lines to the curve

annotations.note: No center-of-curvature definition in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature as limit of arc-tangent angle ratio

anchor.snippet: the curvature at a point P on the curve is the limit of the curvature of sections containing P as the length of the sections approaches zero

annotations.note: No curvature-as-limit definition in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature is parametrization-independent

anchor.snippet: Curvature is a differential-geometric property of the curve; it does not depend on the parametrization of the curve

annotations.note: No statement about parametrization-independence of curvature in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Arc-length parametrization

anchor.snippet: A curve that is parametrized by arc length is a vector-valued function that is denoted by the Greek letter gamma with an overbar

annotations.note: Grep for arcLength/arc_length in Mathlib4 returns no results.

annotations.label: First derivative of arc-length parametrization is unit tangent

anchor.snippet: the first derivative of – γ , – γ ′( s ) is a unit tangent vector

annotations.note: No arc-length parametrization or unit-tangent formalization found in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Second derivative is the curvature vector

anchor.snippet: the second derivative of – γ is T ′( s ) , which is also the curvature vector

annotations.note: No curvature vector defined in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature as magnitude of second derivative

anchor.snippet: Curvature is the magnitude of the second derivative of – γ

annotations.note: No curvature definition via second derivative in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Re-parametrization by arc length

anchor.snippet: can be re-parametrized by arc length provided that γ ′(t) is everywhere not zero

annotations.note: No arc-length reparametrization construction found in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Arc-length parameter

anchor.snippet: The arc-length parameter, s , is defined by

annotations.note: No arc-length parameter definition for smooth curves found in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Same curvature under reparametrization

anchor.snippet: Both γ and – γ trace the same path in

annotations.note: No reparametrization invariance for curvature in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature vector as perpendicular component

anchor.snippet: The curvature vector, K , is the perpendicular component of γ ′′( t ) / ‖ γ ′( t ) ‖ 2 relative to the tangent vector

annotations.note: No curvature vector formula in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Continuity of curvature under differentiability

anchor.snippet: If γ is twice continuously differentiable, then so is s(t) and – γ

annotations.note: No such statement formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature vector

anchor.snippet: The curvature vector, denoted with an upper-case K , is the derivative of the unit tangent vector

annotations.note: No curvature vector definition in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Properties of the curvature vector

anchor.snippet: The curvature vector has the following properties

annotations.note: No curvature vector properties formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature vector points toward center of osculating circle

anchor.snippet: The curvature vector points from the point on the curve, P , in the direction of the center of the osculating circle

annotations.note: No osculating circle in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Tangent and curvature vectors span osculating plane

anchor.snippet: The curvature vector and the tangent vector are perpendicular vectors that span the osculating plane

annotations.note: No osculating plane construction in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Unit normal vector

anchor.snippet: The curvature vector scaled to unit length is the unit normal vector

annotations.note: No unit normal vector for curves in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature vector is parametrization-independent

anchor.snippet: The curvature vector is a differential-geometric property of the curve at P ; it does not depend on how the curve is parametrized

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Osculating circle

anchor.snippet: the curvature of a differentiable curve was defined through the osculating circle , which is the circle that best approximates the curve at a point

annotations.note: No osculating circle defined in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Radius of curvature

anchor.snippet: The radius of curvature, R , is the reciprocal of the curvature

annotations.note: No radius-of-curvature definition in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Center of curvature

anchor.snippet: since a non-zero curvature vector , K ( t ) , points from the point P = γ ( t ) towards the center of curvature

annotations.note: No center-of-curvature concept in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature from arc and chord length

anchor.section: Curvature from arc and chord length
anchor.snippet: The curvature of γ at P is given by the limit

annotations.note: No arc/chord curvature limit definition in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Piecewise curve with differing one-sided derivatives

anchor.snippet: if the one-sided derivatives for γ ′( t 0 ) exist but are different values

annotations.note: No related curvature exception examples in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Indeterminate curvature evaluated via l'Hôpital's rule

anchor.snippet: Another situation occurs when the limit of a ratio results in an indeterminate 0 / 0 value for the curvature

annotations.note: No such curvature example formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature of a circle is 1/R

anchor.snippet: A geometric explanation for why the curvature of a circle of radius R at any point P is 1/ R

annotations.note: Circle curvature not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Circle via standard parametrization

anchor.snippet: A common parametrization of a circle of radius r is γ ( t ) = ( r cos t , r sin t )

annotations.note: Circle parametrization exists in Mathlib4 but not as a curvature example here.

annotations.label: Arc-length parametrization of the circle

anchor.snippet: The circle is a rare case where the arc-length parametrization is easy to compute

annotations.note: No arc-length parametrization framework in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Circle defined implicitly

anchor.snippet: The same circle can also be defined by the implicit equation

annotations.note: Implicit-curve curvature example not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Signed curvature of a parabola

anchor.snippet: Consider the parabola y = ax 2 + bx + c

annotations.note: Parabola signed curvature example not formalized in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Maximum curvature at parabola's vertex

anchor.snippet: The (unsigned) curvature is maximal for x = – ⁠ b / 2 a ⁠ , that is at the stationary point

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Parabola via general parametrization

anchor.snippet: Consider the parametrization γ ( t ) = ( t , at 2 + bt + c ) = ( x , y )

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Parabola defined implicitly

anchor.snippet: The same parabola can also be defined by the implicit equation

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Proper parametric representation of a plane curve

anchor.snippet: Here proper means that on the domain of definition of the parametrization, the derivative

annotations.note: No proper-parametric-representation concept in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature formula for plane curves

anchor.snippet: The curvature κ of a plane curve can be expressed in ways that are specific to two dimensions

annotations.note: Plane curve curvature formula not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Coordinate-free expression of plane curve curvature

anchor.snippet: This can be expressed in a coordinate-free way as

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Signed curvature

anchor.snippet: it can be useful to express the curvature as a single scalar that can be positive or negative, called the signed curvature

annotations.note: No signed curvature definition in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Sign of k via orientation

anchor.snippet: Whether k is positive or negative depends on the orientation of the curve

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature vector as product of signed curvature and unit normal

anchor.snippet: the curvature vector, K is equal to the product of the signed curvature, k and

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Center of curvature via signed curvature

anchor.snippet: Similarly, the center of curvature can be expressed using the signed curvature

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature of a graph y = f(x)

anchor.snippet: The graph of a function y = f ( x ) , is a special case of a parametrized curve

annotations.note: No graph curvature formula in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Sign of signed curvature equals sign of f''

anchor.snippet: The sign of the signed curvature is the same as the sign of the second derivative of f

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4 (signed curvature undefined there).

annotations.label: Small-slope approximation by the second derivative

anchor.snippet: When the slope of the graph (that is the derivative of the function) is small, the signed curvature is well approximated by the second derivative

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature of an implicit curve

anchor.snippet: For a curve defined by an implicit equation F ( x , y ) = 0 with partial derivatives

annotations.note: No implicit-curve curvature formula in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Signed curvature undefined for implicit curves

anchor.snippet: The signed curvature is not defined, as it depends on an orientation of the curve that is not provided by the implicit equation

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Singular point of an implicit curve

anchor.snippet: A point of the curve where F x = F y = 0 is a singular point

annotations.note: No implicit-curve singular-point definition in Mathlib4 in this differential-geometric sense.

annotations.label: Curvature in polar coordinates

anchor.snippet: If a curve is defined in polar coordinates by the radius expressed as a function of the polar angle

annotations.note: No polar-coordinate curvature formula in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature comb

anchor.snippet: A curvature comb

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Continuity levels G0–G3

anchor.snippet: The continuity can be defined as being in one of four levels

annotations.note: Geometric G0–G3 continuity not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: First Frenet–Serret formula

anchor.snippet: The first Frenet–Serret formula relates the unit tangent vector, curvature, and the normal vector of an arc-length parametrization

annotations.note: Grep for 'Frenet' returns no Mathlib4 hits.

annotations.label: Second Frenet–Serret formula for planar curves

anchor.snippet: As planar curves have zero torsion , the second Frenet–Serret formula provides the relation

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Frenet–Serret in general parametrization

anchor.snippet: For a general parametrization by a parameter t , one needs expressions involving derivatives with respect to t

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Curvature of a space curve

anchor.snippet: For a parametrically defined curve in three dimensions given in Cartesian coordinates

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Cross product formula for space curves

anchor.snippet: Both the curvature

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Normal curvature, geodesic curvature, geodesic torsion

anchor.snippet: several curvatures are defined, which relates the direction of curvature to the surface's unit normal vector

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Darboux frame

anchor.snippet: let the curve be arc-length parametrized , and let t = u × T so that T , t , u form an orthonormal basis , called the Darboux frame

annotations.note: No Darboux frame in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Principal curvatures and directions

anchor.snippet: the maximum and minimum values of the normal curvature at a point are called the principal curvatures

annotations.note: No principal curvature definition in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Developable surface

anchor.snippet: Such developable surfaces have zero Gaussian curvature

annotations.note: No developable surface definition in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Gaussian curvature

anchor.snippet: The Gaussian curvature , named after Carl Friedrich Gauss , is equal to the product of the principal curvatures

annotations.note: No Gaussian curvature defined in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Gaussian curvature is intrinsic

anchor.snippet: Gaussian curvature is an intrinsic property of the surface, meaning it does not depend on the particular embedding of the surface

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Theorema Egregium

anchor.snippet: This is Gauss 's celebrated Theorema Egregium

annotations.note: No Theorema Egregium in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Bertrand–Diguet–Puiseux theorem

anchor.snippet: the Gaussian curvature K at the point P can be computed by the Bertrand–Diguet–Puiseux theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib's 'Bertrand' file is the prime postulate, unrelated to this theorem.

annotations.label: Gauss–Bonnet theorem

anchor.snippet: The integral of the Gaussian curvature over the whole surface is closely related to the surface's Euler characteristic

annotations.note: Grep for Gauss-Bonnet returns no Mathlib4 hits.

annotations.label: Descartes' theorem on total angular defect

anchor.snippet: the analog for the Gauss–Bonnet theorem is Descartes' theorem on total angular defect

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Mean curvature

anchor.snippet: The mean curvature is an extrinsic measure of curvature equal to half the sum of the principal curvatures

annotations.note: No mean curvature definition in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Second fundamental form

anchor.snippet: The intrinsic and extrinsic curvature of a surface can be combined in the second fundamental form

annotations.note: No second fundamental form in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Principal axis form of the second fundamental form

anchor.snippet: by the principal axis theorem , the second fundamental form is

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Shape operator

anchor.snippet: An encapsulation of surface curvature can be found in the shape operator, S , which is a self-adjoint linear operator from the tangent plane to itself

annotations.note: No shape operator in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Weingarten equations

anchor.snippet: The Weingarten equations give the value of S in terms of the coefficients of the first and second fundamental forms

annotations.note: No Weingarten equations in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Principal curvatures as eigenvalues of shape operator

anchor.snippet: The principal curvatures are the eigenvalues of the shape operator

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Intrinsic curvature of higher-dimensional space

anchor.snippet: a space of three or more dimensions can be intrinsically curved

annotations.note: Not in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Flat space

anchor.snippet: A space or space-time with zero curvature is called flat

annotations.note: No Riemannian 'flat space' definition (in this sense) in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Sectional curvature as tidal force

anchor.snippet: curvature in higher dimensions can be regarded as a kind of tidal force

annotations.note: No sectional curvature in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: Holonomy

anchor.snippet: This phenomenon is known as holonomy

annotations.note: Grep for 'holonomy' returns no Mathlib4 hits.

annotations.label: Scalar curvature and Ricci curvature

anchor.snippet: Two more generalizations of curvature are the scalar curvature and Ricci curvature

annotations.note: No Ricci or scalar curvature in Mathlib4.

annotations.label: CAT(k) spaces

anchor.snippet: The notion of a triangle makes senses in metric spaces , and this gives rise to CAT( k ) spaces

annotations.note: No CAT(k) spaces formalized in Mathlib4.
